Chapter 01 — Foundation / Sales Process Overview

Sales Process Overview

Training Tool Universal Framework
How to Use This Section
This is an education and training tool — it teaches you how the process works. You won't reference it in real-time during calls. For live call scripts, use the Leads Pipeline or Intro Offer Pipeline sections.

Sales at STRONG is about solving a problem. Every person who reaches out has something they're trying to fix, change, or achieve. Our job is to understand that problem well enough to offer the right solution. You can't solve a problem you don't understand — that's why discovery sits at the heart of any sales interaction.

The flow is designed to feel like a natural conversation, not a script you're reading from — whether you're on the phone with a brand-new lead, following up with someone mid-Intro Offer, or speaking with someone face to face. The key is understanding the purpose of each stage so you can move through them fluidly. Forcing any stage kills momentum and trust. Let the conversation lead you through.

The Sales Flow
Stage 1
Intro
~30 sec
Control
Stage 2
Discovery
5–10 min
Stage 3
Options
2–3 min
Stage 4
Recommend
5–10 min
Trial Close
Stage 5
Objections
As needed
Stage 6
Close
3–5 min
Stage 1 — Introduction
Goal: Welcome the lead, establish a valid reason for the call, and transition seamlessly into discovery.
Structure
  • Greeting + your name — clear, confident, no hesitation
  • Thank them for registering or downloading the app
  • Valid Business Reason — "Quick welcome call, answer any questions"
  • Immediate pivot into discovery — don't pause and wait
Example Script
Opening Script
"Hi [Name], this is [Your Name] from STRONG Pilates. Thanks for setting up your account with us — just wanted to give you a quick welcome call and see if you had any questions. I'm curious though, what made you look into STRONG?"
Key Points
  • Keep it to 30 seconds max — you're not there yet, don't overstay
  • Never ask permission to continue: no "Is now a good time?"
  • Don't force rapport — small talk before you've earned trust feels hollow. Rapport builds through discovery
  • The final sentence of your intro IS the first discovery question. Don't separate them
Rule
The introduction isn't about you. It's a runway to get the lead talking. The moment they answer your opening question, you're in discovery — and that's where the real work begins.
The Principle
You are guiding this conversation — not being pulled through it. At every stage you control the pace, the direction, and when topics get addressed. A lead who jumps ahead isn't a problem. It's your job to acknowledge and redirect without losing your thread.
Why it matters
Answering every question as it comes up mid-discovery means you're in objection handling before you've built any value. You lose the thread, the lead controls the pace, and you're playing catch-up. Holding your structure signals confidence — you know where you're going.
How it works
Acknowledge the question, touch on it briefly if needed, then redirect back to where you were. Park it with a commitment to come back — and then come back to it. The lead feels heard, and you stay in control of the structure.
Acknowledge → Touch → Redirect
Price asked too early
Lead jumps to pricing before you've covered discovery or value.
"Absolutely — I'll go through all of that with you. I just want to understand what you're after first so I can point you in the right direction. Can I ask..."
Question mid-discovery
Lead raises a concern or question while you're still building their picture.
"Good question — let me come back to that in a second. I just want to get a bit more of an understanding of your situation so I can give you the right answer."
Interruption mid-recommendation
Lead cuts in with a question while you're presenting the recommendation.
"Great point — I'm actually going to cover that right now. Bear with me for one second..."
— The Rule
Always acknowledge, never ignore. But the question gets answered at the right time — not whenever it's asked.
Stage 2 — Discovery
Goal: Understand the lead's situation deeply enough to make a recommendation that actually fits them.
This stage has its own dedicated section with full question guides, response tables, and best practices. CLICK HERE TO SEE: DISCOVERY PROCESS (CHAPTER 01, SECTION 03) → The overview below is a summary only.
The 5 Core Discovery Questions
# Question What You're Learning Skip If...
Q1 "What made you look into STRONG?" Core motivation, goals, what they're attracted to Never — always start here
Q2 "Are you doing any training at the moment?" Experience level, current routine, injuries Already covered naturally in Q1
Q3 "Has anything stopped you from starting before?" Barriers, past failures, objections to address early Momentum is very high and they're clearly ready
Q4 "How many sessions a week would you ideally want to do?" Product fit — determines pack vs membership recommendation Never — you need frequency before recommending
Q5 "When were you thinking of getting started?" Urgency, timeline, readiness to commit Never — you need a start date before recommending
Discovery Rules
  • Make it a non-negotiable to cover all five questions — in some conversations you may not be able to, but it's important and fundamental to this stage of the sale
  • If you can't get all five, you need at minimum goal (Q1), frequency (Q4) and start date (Q5) before moving to Stage 3
  • Questions should flow naturally — don't rapid-fire them
  • Take notes. Goals, barriers, experience level, schedule, budget sensitivity
  • Let them talk. Silence is not awkward — it means they're thinking
  • Rapport builds here, through genuine curiosity. Not in the intro
Discovery Completion Checklist
⚠️
Before moving to Stage 3 — confirm you have all five:
Primary goal/motivation · Fitness experience level · Any barriers or past failures · Ideal weekly session frequency · Intended start date. If you're missing any of these, ask before recommending. At minimum you need goal, frequency and start date.
Stage 3 — Options
Goal: Give the lead a sense of choice before making your recommendation. The two options aren't a menu — they come directly from what the person told you in discovery. Their goals, frequency, experience, and barriers give you a picture of what they actually need. From that picture you identify two options: the ideal (the best fit for what they described) and the workable (a legitimate option that would serve them — just not as well as the ideal). Some people genuinely need the workable option, and that's the right call for them. But presenting both means the ideal has a reference point — and in most cases, that context makes the right choice clear. Present both neutrally. The recommendation comes in Stage 4.
💡
The options you present should feel like a natural response to the conversation — not a product menu. If you've done discovery properly, the right two options will be obvious. If they're not, go back and ask more questions.
Leads Pipeline — Presenting Options
Example Script
"So there are a couple of ways you can get started with us. You could book in for a casual session — that's $35 and gives you a single class to try it out. Or there's our Intro Offer — 5 sessions for $50 over 14 days, so you get to experience a few different classes before committing to anything. Which of those sounds more like what you had in mind?"
Example Script
"Because you mentioned you're pretty new to this, there are two ways you could get started. We run a free Starter Class every Saturday at 11am — it's specifically for beginners, so you'd learn the equipment and the movements in a really low-pressure environment. Or there's our Intro Offer — 5 sessions for $50 over 14 days, which gives you more time to find your feet across a few different classes. How does each of those sound to you?"
Intro Offer Pipeline — Presenting Membership Options
Example Script
"So based on what you've told me, there are two membership options worth looking at. There's our 2-sessions-a-week membership — $50 a week, no contract, completely flexible. Or there's our Unlimited on a 6-month contract at $70 a week, which gets you into as many classes as you want each week. Do either of those feel like they'd suit where you're at?"
Rule
Never present more than two options. More than two creates decision paralysis. Option A exists to make Option B feel like the clear right choice — not to give them a genuine dilemma. Present both options neutrally, get their response, then move into Stage 4 to make your recommendation.
Stage 4 — Recommendation
Goal: Make the recommendation. Options set the context — don't re-establish it. You're not summarising what they told you again. You're picking one of the two options and telling them why, directly and confidently. The recommendation should feel like a verdict, not a recap.
Recommendation Framework
  • Lead with the call — open with the recommendation directly. "Of those two, I'd go with the Intro Offer." Don't re-summarise discovery before landing it.
  • Give the reason — one or two sentences connecting the recommendation to their situation. Features → benefit → their specific need.
  • Invite their response — close with "How does that sound?" Don't leave silence hanging.
💡
Options already reflected discovery back to them. Recommend picks up from there. If you find yourself opening with "based on what you told me" here, you're re-doing work that Stage 3 already did.
Quick Reference — Leads Pipeline
Example Script
"Of those two, I'd go with the Intro Offer. 5 sessions over 14 days gives you enough time to actually feel the difference, try a few different class types, and figure out what works for you. At $50 it's the lowest-risk way to get started properly. How does that sound?"
Example Script
"Given everything you've told me, I'd actually start with the Starter Class. It's free, it's on Saturday at 11am, and it's specifically designed so you're not walking into a full class cold. You'll learn the equipment, get comfortable with the movements, and by the end of it you'll know exactly whether this is for you — before spending a cent. Does that feel like the right starting point?"
Example Script
"I'd go with the Intro Offer over the casual. You're getting 5 sessions for $50 — that's $10 a class — compared to $35 for a single session. And you've got 14 days to use them, so there's no pressure to rush. It's by far the better value if you're thinking about doing more than one class. Does that make sense?"
Quick Reference — Intro Offer Pipeline
Example Script
"I'd go with the Unlimited. You told me you want to train 3 times a week — at $70 a week that's about $23 a class. The 2/week membership works out to $25 a class and you'd be capped at 2 sessions, which means you'd either have to hold back or pay extra whenever you wanted a third. Based on your goals and how often you want to train, Unlimited just makes more sense. How does that feel?"
Example Script
"I'd go with the 2/week membership. You've said 2 sessions a week is what you're after — it's $50 a week, no contract, so you're not locked into anything. The Unlimited gives you more flexibility but you'd be paying $70 a week for sessions you're not going to use. This one fits exactly where you're at right now. Does that work for you?"
Example Script
"Since you need flexibility, a class pack might work better for you. The 10-pack is $299 and gives you 60 days to use them — so you can book when it suits your schedule. Does that feel like a better fit?"
These are summary examples only. For 10+ detailed recommendation scenarios with full scripting for both pipelines, see Chapter 04 — Recommendations.
What it is
A check-in question you can use at any point in the call to test alignment, confirm understanding, or gauge where the lead is before moving forward. It's not the actual close — it's a pulse check. You're not asking for a decision, you're asking for a temperature.
Why you always do it
Trial closes aren't just for surfacing objections — they're moments of affirmation and clarity. Use them to check alignment, confirm the lead is with you, and help solidify their own thinking as the conversation moves forward. A lead who has affirmed their direction multiple times throughout the call is far more likely to commit at the close.
How it works
After your recommendation, ask a simple open check-in. Their response determines the pace of Stage 4 — if they're clear, Stage 5 is brief. If something surfaces, you handle it before moving to Stage 6. Either way, Stage 5 always happens.
Trial Close Techniques
If...Then
Tests whether the stated objection is the real one or a smokescreen.
"If we could find a time that works for your schedule, would you be comfortable moving forward?"
Scale Question
Opens up the conversation naturally. Anything under 8 tells you there's something to address.
"On a scale of 1–10, how are you feeling about giving it a go?"
Assumptive Check-In
Gets a verbal confirmation without asking for the sale.
"So it sounds like this could work for you — is that fair to say?"
Directional Question
Used mid-discovery or mid-recommendation to confirm they're following and aligned.
"Does that make sense so far?"
Pain-to-Solution Bridge
Connects their own words back to your recommendation. Hard to say no to.
"You mentioned [their goal] is important to you — does what I've described feel like it would get you there?"
The Clarifier
Used when you sense hesitation but nothing has been voiced. Surfaces hidden objections before the close.
"Is there anything stopping you from getting started?"
Also works as a trial close — Alternate Close
The alternate close isn't just a closing technique — it can be used mid-call as a trial close to establish clarity, capture affirmation, and solidify the lead's thinking. Use the product alt, start date alt, or both in sequence. Works on smooth conversations just as well as hesitant ones.
Product: "Are you happy to get the [Product] sorted, or would you prefer to start with the [Product]?"
Start date: "Did you want to get started this week or next?"
— Mandatory Trial Close — End of Stage 4
"How does that sound?"
"Do you have any questions?"
Confirm they understood and are aligned with what you just presented
Give them an explicit opening to raise anything before you move to the close
If something isn't locked in, you find out now rather than at the close
Stage 5 — Objections
Goal: Address concerns in a way that builds trust rather than creates pressure.

Objections are not rejections. They're almost always a signal that something wasn't fully addressed in discovery, or that the lead needs more information before they feel confident committing. The best way to reduce objections is to run better discovery — if you've already surfaced a concern in Q3, you've had the chance to address it before it becomes a wall.

When an objection comes up after your recommendation, work through it using the four-step approach below. Never argue, never steamroll, and never make the lead feel judged for pushing back.

The 4-Step Objection Approach
  • Acknowledge — validate the concern without agreeing it's a problem. "That makes sense" or "I hear you." Never skip this step — it defuses defensiveness
  • Explore — ask one follow-up question to understand the real concern behind the objection. "What is it about the cost specifically?" or "What does your schedule look like?" Don't assume you know what they mean
  • Address — respond directly to what they told you in the explore step, not the surface-level objection. Use facts, not pressure
  • Reconfirm — check in before moving forward. "Does that help?" or "Does that make sense?" Then move toward the close
Rule
One objection at a time. Address it fully before moving on. If you rush, they feel steamrolled. If you skip the explore step, you're answering the wrong question.
Common Objections — Quick Reference
Objection What it Usually Means How to Approach It
"It's a bit expensive" Price sensitivity or needs value reframed. Rarely means "I can't afford it" Explore what expensive means to them. Break down cost per class. Reframe against what they're currently spending on fitness (or not spending)
"I need to think about it" Missing information, not fully convinced, or another objection underneath Ask what's making them want to think it over — there's almost always a specific concern. Give it shape rather than letting it sit open
"I'm not sure I can commit to sessions" Schedule uncertainty. May be using this as a reason to delay Ask about their typical week. If the schedule is genuinely unpredictable, acknowledge it and redirect toward flexibility options (pack vs membership)
"I need to check with my partner" Budget decision made jointly, or using a third party as a delay Acknowledge it's reasonable. Ask: "Is cost the main thing you'd want to discuss with them, or is there something else?" Try to progress the decision without dismissing the concern
"I just want to try a class first" Uncertainty about the product — needs a lower-risk first step This is a genuine and reasonable request. Offer the free Starter Class if they're a beginner, or lean into the Intro Offer as the low-risk first step it already is
Price Objection — Example Script
Example Script
"That's completely fair — it's worth making sure it fits. Can I ask what feels expensive about it — is it the upfront cost, or the ongoing weekly commitment?" [Listen.] "Got it. So the Intro Offer is $50 total — that's 5 sessions over 14 days, which works out to $10 a class. There's no ongoing commitment attached to it — it's genuinely just a way to get in, try it, and see if it works for you before making any bigger decision. Does that feel more manageable as a starting point?"
Example Script
"Of course — it's worth feeling good about it. Can I ask what the main thing is you're weighing up? Is it the cost, the timing, or something else?" [Listen.] "That makes sense. [Address the specific concern.] Is there anything else that's holding you back, or is it mainly that?"
Example Script
"Completely understand — that's a fair thing to do. Is cost the main thing you'd want to run past them, or is there something else on your mind?" [Listen.] "That makes sense. If it helps, the Intro Offer is only $50 — there's no ongoing commitment attached to it, so it's a pretty easy one to say yes to without needing a big conversation. Would it be worth locking it in now and having the bigger membership conversation after you've had a chance to try a few sessions?"
Key Points
  • The best objection prevention is thorough discovery — Q3 exists for this reason
  • Always acknowledge before addressing — skipping this makes you sound defensive
  • Explore the specific concern before responding — don't assume what they mean
  • If an objection persists after two attempts, don't push. Move to Stage 6 and secure a next step
This is a summary of the approach. For the full objection library with detailed handling scripts for every scenario, see Chapter 06 — Objections & Handling.
Stage 6 — Close
Goal: Secure a commitment or a clear next step — never leave a call without one.

Closing is not a high-pressure technique. A good close is the natural conclusion to a good conversation — when you've understood the lead's situation and presented something that genuinely fits, asking for the commitment should feel like the obvious next step.

Every call ends with a commitment or a clear next step. That might be a purchase, a booked class, a follow-up call, or a specific date to reconnect. A vague "I'll think about it" is not an outcome — your job is to give it shape before you hang up.

The Ask

Once objections are handled and the lead is aligned, ask directly. Don't wait for them to volunteer a yes — that moment rarely comes on its own. The ask mirrors back what they've told you, assumes forward motion, and gives them a soft out.

  • Mirror first when you can — if there's any ambiguity about what they're committing to, confirm the product, timing, and frequency before asking. It grounds the ask in their own words and makes the close feel like a natural conclusion. Default to this.
  • Skip the mirror when momentum is there — if the conversation has been clear and they're ready, the mirror adds friction. Go straight to the ask.
Ask Scripts
Example Script
"Okay, so just confirming — you're leaning towards the Intro Offer, wanting to start next week and get a few sessions in. Are you happy to get that sorted now, or was there anything else you wanted to cover?"
Example Script
"Are you happy to get that sorted now, or was there anything else you wanted to cover?"
💡
The alternate close is a tool for clarity, affirmation, and momentum. Use it to establish which product they want, lock in timing, or solidify the decision in their own mind. You can use the product alt, the start date alt, or both in sequence. Works on smooth sales just as well as hesitant ones.
Example Script
"Are you happy to get the Intro Offer sorted, or would you prefer to start with the Starter Class?"
Silence
After the ask, stop talking. Give the lead space to think. They may need a moment to get clear on how they feel, what questions they still have, or whether they're ready to commit. That's okay — that's exactly what this pause is for. Don't rush it. The average salesperson fills the gap within 2–3 seconds; research shows prospects need closer to 8–10 to formulate a real response. If you jump in too early you'll cut off whatever they were about to tell you — and that might have been a yes, a concern worth addressing, or the one question that needed answering before they could commit. Let the silence do its job.
Two Types of Close
Outcome When It Applies What It Looks Like
Commitment Close Lead is ready — objections resolved, offer accepted, ready to go Purchase confirmed, class booked, membership started. Get the name and card details or send the booking link before you hang up
Next Step Close Lead isn't ready to commit today but conversation is still warm Specific follow-up date and time locked in before ending the call. "I'll call you Thursday at 10am" — not "I'll follow up later"
Commitment Close — Example Scripts
Close Script
"Great — I can get that set up for you now. The Intro Offer is $50 for 5 sessions over 14 days. I'll send you through the booking link and you can lock in your first session. What's the best email to send that to?"
Close Script
"Brilliant. I'll get the [membership type] set up now — that kicks off at $[price] per week from your next billing date. I'll send through the agreement for you to sign off on, and we'll have you booked in. Does [day/time] work for your next session?"
Next Step Close — When They're Not Ready Today
Next Step Script
"No worries at all — take the time you need. When do you think you'll have had a chance to think it over? I can give you a call [day] if that works?" [Confirm specific day and time.] "Perfect — I'll touch base with you then. In the meantime, feel free to have a look at the timetable on the app and let me know if you have any questions."
Closing Principles
  • Always quote retail pricing first — introduce incentives only after the standard price has been presented
  • Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount. Stacking is permitted as the final play
  • An objection is not the end of the close — handle it in Stage 5 first, then come back to the close
  • If they're not ready today, get a specific date and time — never let "I'll think about it" sit open
  • Always book or confirm the next touchpoint before ending the call — whether it's a session time or a follow-up call
⚠️
"I'll think about it" is not an outcome. Every call ends with either a purchase or a concrete next step with a specific date and time. If you hang up without one of these, the lead is as good as lost.
Universal Process Rules
Non-Negotiables
  • Never ask permission to continue — "Is now a good time?" kills momentum
  • No forced rapport in the introduction — it feels hollow before trust is built
  • Summary happens inside the recommendation, not before it as a separate step
  • Always quote retail pricing first — never lead with a discount
  • Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount (stacking permitted as final play)
  • You must have frequency and start date before recommending any product
  • Every call ends with a commitment or a concrete next step
How This Applies to Each Pipeline

The sales flow is universal — but how you move through it depends on which pipeline you're working. The pipelines aren't different processes, they're different contexts for the same process.

Pipeline Typical Flow What Changes
Leads Intro → Discovery → Recommend Intro Offer → Objections → Close Full discovery every time. Goal is always to sell the Intro Offer (or Starter Class as fallback)
Leads — Other Promos Intro → Discovery → Recommend Promo Offer → Objections → Close Same as Leads but offer name/terms change. Never run concurrent with standard Intro Offer
Intro Offer — Scenario A Nurture → Book Sessions → Convert Discovery already done on Leads call. Skip straight to nurture and booking
Intro Offer — Scenario B Discovery → Nurture → Book → Convert Self-purchased with no prior contact. Full discovery needed before anything else
Intro Offer — Scenario C Fill gaps → Nurture → Book → Convert Partial discovery from first call. Fill gaps, then proceed to nurture and booking
Full pipeline scripts, CRM workflows, and nurture sequences are in Chapter 03 — Pipelines.
Chapter 01 — Foundation / What is STRONG?

What is STRONG?

Brand Level Education + Sales Tool All Locations
How to Use This Section
This is Brand Level content — it applies to every STRONG studio nationally. Read it to understand what STRONG is and why it works. Use the "How to explain it" prompts to talk about STRONG confidently on calls, in-studio, or face to face.
STRONG is more than Pilates.

Reformer Pilates meets cardio, resistance, and strength training — delivered on custom-built machines in a 45-minute class that pushes limits without punishing your body. The result is a low-impact, high-intensity workout that members keep coming back to.

ROW RIDE REFORM
The Product

STRONG is a fitness movement that combines rowing or riding with Pilates-inspired movements, using the custom-built Rowformer and Bikeformer machines. Classes are 45 minutes — not every class is cardio-based, some are more strength-focused, some are pure Pilates. What makes it different is the programming — classes follow expert-designed 12-week cycles with 4-week progressive blocks, building week by week so members are actually making progress that's intentful and by design.

How to explain STRONG simply "It's Pilates, but more intense than what most people picture. We use custom-built machines — the Rowformer and the Bikeformer — that combine Pilates-based movements with rowing or riding. Classes are 45 minutes and low-impact on your joints. If you're just starting out, you can absolutely pace yourself — but with a bit of experience under your belt they'll really push you. Not every class is cardio-based either — some are more strength-focused, some are pure Pilates. What makes it different is the programming — every class is part of a 12-week cycle that builds week by week, so you're actually making progress, and that's intentful and by design."
Common question — "How does the rowing/riding work?" "The Rowformer is a reformer with a rower built onto one end — you move between the reformer and the erg. The Bikeformer is a separate bike that sits next to the reformer."
Key Points
  • Not all classes use the erg — some are pure strength or Pilates-based
  • Low-impact on joints doesn't mean low-intensity — the challenge is real
  • Programming is 12-week structured cycles, not random workouts
  • New members can pace themselves in any class — there's no single entry point
Rule
Never describe STRONG as "just Pilates". It's a distinct training methodology. The machines, the programming, and the intensity are what set it apart — that's your story to tell on every call.
Brand Values

STRONG is built around four core values. These shape the culture, the coaching approach, and the experience members have in every class. Knowing these helps you speak about STRONG with authenticity — not just features and prices.

Progression
Perfection is unrealistic, unattainable, and uninspiring, while progression is a constant process: one we opt into every day and one that never ends. There's always a new goal to achieve and another mountain to conquer. We're hungry, thirsty, and consistently trying to do better and be better.
Collaboration
We work together to achieve better. In our group training, we lift up those beside us and are lifted in return. We also connect with people who possess skills we don't. From DJs and perfumologists, to professional rowing instructors and every STRONG human. We're humbled by the fact that we're not always the best in the room: everyone has something new to offer.
Holistic Living
We believe things are deeply connected: like the parts of our bodies and the components of our workouts. To benefit each individual part, we view the thing in its entirety and all the factors that impact it.
Substance
There's more than what meets the eye. We care about more than the surface, but the depth and layers behind our inputs and outputs. There are strong reasons and considerations behind everything we do, from our marketing to our workouts. Our trainers, owners, classes, studios, customers, and ambassadors; they all have substance, and it shows.
💡
Why this matters for sales When a lead says "I've tried other studios and never stuck with it" — the answer lives here. STRONG's values explain why it works when other things haven't: progressive programming, real community, and results that are actually trackable.
The Equipment

STRONG uses a range of equipment across its classes. Knowing what each piece is and what it does helps you describe the experience accurately and handle questions confidently.

Rowformer
STRONG's signature machine — a reformer with a rower built onto one end. Members move between the reformer side and the erg side within a single class. The combination of spring resistance and rowing delivers a full-body workout unlike anything else.
Bikeformer
A separate bike that sits next to the reformer. In classes that use it, members move between the reformer and the Bikeformer erg. Delivers the same low-impact, high-intensity approach but with a cycling focus.
Dumbbells
Four weight ranges available across all studios. Members choose based on the movement and their level — form always comes before load.
Light 2–5kg / 4–11lb
Medium 4–7kg / 8–15lb
Heavy 6–10kg / 13–22lb
Extra Heavy 10kg+ / 22lb+
Rowflex Bands
STRONG's resistance bands — used across all class types to add resistance, support movement patterns, and deepen muscle engagement. Versatile across both strength and Pilates-based movements.
RSS
Reverse Spring System
A world-first innovation on the Rowformer. The RSS loads resistance from underneath the row platform — turning the Rowformer into a hybrid strength machine. Members can lift over 40kg of spring-loaded resistance while keeping all the benefits of spring tension: smooth, controlled, and joint-friendly.
Common question — "How does the rowing/riding work?" "The Rowformer is a reformer with a rower built onto one end — you move between the reformer and the erg. The Bikeformer is a separate bike that sits next to the reformer."
Common question — "Do I need to be strong to do Loaded?" "Not at all — there are four dumbbell ranges so you always work at the right level for you. The coach will guide you on load selection. The priority is always form first, weight second."
Class Types

STRONG offers six class types across the timetable. All classes are designed for all levels — if you're new, you pace yourself, learn the movements, and take your time. Each class has a distinct focus — knowing the difference helps you match a lead to the right starting point and answer questions about what to expect.

STRONG Body
All Rounder
ERG
50%
Pilates Flow
50%

Balanced full-body Pilates inspired training paired with cardio bursts. Each session will push limits and build your strength, mobility, and endurance.

How to explain it
"STRONG Body is the all-rounder — you're getting cardio, strength, and Pilates all in one session. It moves between the erg and the reformer so you get a bit of everything. It's a great place to start but it suits all levels — you just work at your own pace and build from there."
STRONG Loaded
Strength Focus
ERG
20%
Strength
80%

Built for progress and designed around true progressive overload, using heavy springs, dumbbells and controlled tempo to maximise time-under-tension and lean muscle.

How to explain it
"STRONG Loaded is the strength class — it's designed around building muscle, getting stronger, and toning up. It uses dumbbells and heavier resistance with a focus on progressive overload, so you're actually seeing results over time. Less cardio, more strength work."
STRONG 360
Variety + Results
ERG
50%
Pilates
25%
Strength
25%

Bringing together everything we love: Pilates, strength, and cardio, in one dynamic, full-body session, it's designed for variety, connection and results.

How to explain it
"STRONG 360 brings everything together — Pilates, strength, and cardio all in one session. It's our most varied class, every week brings something different. We up the intensity, and if you want variety, connection, and you want results, this is the one."
STRONG Pilates Strength
No Erg
Pilates
50%
Strength
50%

A refined blend of traditional Pilates and strength-based movements, performed in controlled, deliberate sequences – all without cardio.

How to explain it
"STRONG Pilates Strength is where Pilates meets strength training. It's controlled, deliberate, and builds real strength — with progressive overload built in so you're actually getting stronger over time. No cardio, no erg — just focused, precise strength work with a Pilates foundation."
STRONG Pilates Only
No Erg
Pilates
100%

A fusion of dynamic movements and controlled Pilates flows with the use of props to deepen muscle engagement.

How to explain it
"STRONG Pilates Only is a fusion of dynamic movements and controlled Pilates flows — using props to deepen muscle engagement. No erg, no cardio — just pure Pilates. If you love the feel of a traditional Pilates class but want something with a bit more burn, this is it."
STRONG Starter
First-Timers
ERG
50%
Pilates Flow
50%

A focused session designed to help first-timers master the Rowformer and feel confident training the STRONG way.

How to explain it
"STRONG Starter is a focused session designed to help first-timers master the Rowformer and feel confident training the STRONG way. It's the perfect starting point if you've never been on the machine before — our coaches walk you through everything so you can hit the ground running in your regular classes."
Class Quick Reference

Use this table to quickly match a lead's goals or preferences to the right class recommendation.

Class Cardio Intensity Strength Intensity Erg Duration
STRONG Body Moderate Moderate Rowformer or Bikeformer 45 min
STRONG Loaded Low High Rowformer or Bikeformer 40 min
STRONG 360 High Moderate Rowformer or Bikeformer 45 min
STRONG Pilates Strength — Moderate–High No erg 45 min
STRONG Pilates Only — Moderate No erg 45 min
STRONG Starter Low Low Rowformer or Bikeformer —
Default recommendation for new leads All classes suit all levels — new members can start anywhere and pace themselves. STRONG Body is the natural starting point for most leads as it covers cardio, strength, and Pilates in one session. STRONG Starter is the right call if they've never been on the Rowformer and want to get their bearings first.
ERG Efforts

ERG (short for ergometer) is the term STRONG uses for the rowing or riding machine. In classes that include erg work, coaches use five named effort levels to guide intensity. Knowing these helps you explain what a class actually feels like — and answer questions like "how hard is it?"

Effort Output What it feels like
Flush Out <50% Gentle recovery pace. Low effort, promotes blood flow between strength blocks.
Paddle 60% Steady aerobic rhythm. Conversational, relaxed, sustainable. Builds endurance without fatigue.
STRONG 70% Purposeful and powerful. Breathing increases, talking gets harder. The sweet spot for aerobic conditioning.
Push 80% Intensity lifts noticeably. Heart rate climbs, heavy breathing sets in. Used for shorter work sets.
All Out 90%+ Full sprint. Short, maximal efforts. Peak power and output — everything you've got.
How to use this If a lead asks "how hard is the rowing?" you can explain that coaches guide intensity using these named effort levels — so you're never just told to "go harder", you're given a clear target. It makes the cardio component structured and trackable, not just suffer-fest rowing.
The Programming

STRONG's programming is what separates it from a regular fitness class. Members aren't just turning up to move — they're training inside a science-backed framework designed for real, progressive results.

Every 12-week cycle is divided into three 4-week blocks (A, B, C), each with a specific focus that builds on the last. Within each block, every week has a clear intent — so the programming evolves deliberately, not randomly.

Weeks 1–4
Block A
Foundation to performance. Learn the patterns, build the base. Week by week progression from stability → load → volume → execution.
Weeks 5–8
Block B
Confidence to capacity. Familiar movements go heavier. Unilateral work exposes weaknesses. ERG builds the engine for what's coming.
Weeks 9–12
Block C
Power and control. Movements repeat intentionally with heavier loads. Turn everything built in A and B into real, usable strength.

STRONG Loaded runs on Mondays (lower body bias) and Fridays (upper body bias). Here's how Block A progresses across its weeks:

Week Focus What that means in practice
Week 1 Foundation Learning the patterns. Slower tempo, lighter loads. Priority is range of motion and control.
Week 2 Stability Unilateral variations — split stance, single-arm. Challenges coordination and balance under load.
Week 3 Loading Heavier dumbbells, reduced rest, compound sets. Strength endurance starts to build.
Week 4 Capacity Higher volume, longer time-under-tension, supersets. Tests aerobic and muscular endurance without losing form.
Week 5 Execution Ties it all together. Moving better with the strength that's been built — precision and flow under fatigue.
💡
This same logic applies across all three blocks — each one builds on the last. By the end of the 12-week cycle, members are noticeably stronger, more stable, and more confident across every class type.
How to explain the programming "What makes STRONG different is that the programming is actually structured. Every class is part of a 12-week cycle divided into blocks — each block has a specific focus, and each week within that block builds on the last. So you're not just repeating the same workout — you're genuinely progressing. That's intentful and by design."
Five Business Pillars Western Sydney Framework
Western Sydney Framework — Not Official STRONG HQ Material The Five Business Pillars are the operational framework we use across Western Sydney studios. They're informed by STRONG's culture but are not official HQ-published content. New staff: this is how our team thinks about the business.

Everything we do at STRONG sits on five pillars. These aren't just business categories — they're the lens through which every team member should think about their role. Understanding them helps you give better service, have better conversations, and understand why certain things matter.

Pillar 01
Results Business
Members pay for outcomes — body recomposition, strength, conditioning, confidence, and energy. Every interaction should connect back to their goal.
Pillar 02
Experience Business
The vibe, the coaching, the music, the energy, the cleanliness, the presentation. Every touchpoint is part of the experience members are paying for.
Pillar 03
Community Business
Members stay because of connection — to coaches, to culture, and to each other. Community is the most powerful retention tool we have.
Pillar 04
Education Business
Movement education and basic nutrition guidance. Empowering members to understand their body and own their health — not just follow instructions.
Pillar 05
Sales & Service Business
We grow by selling well and serving better. Sales isn't separate from service — it's an expression of it. When you genuinely understand what a member needs and connect them to the right product, that's the job done right.
Chapter 01 — Foundation / Discovery Process

Discovery Process

Deep Dive All Pipelines
Deep dive on Stage 2 — Discovery from the Sales Process Overview

Discovery is where you earn the right to recommend. Before you suggest anything — an offer, a membership, a starting point — you need to understand what the person in front of you actually wants, what's held them back before, and what their life looks like right now. The better your discovery, the fewer objections you'll face later. Most objections in the close are questions that weren't answered in discovery.

There are five core questions. You won't always ask all five verbatim — some answers come up naturally in conversation. Make it a non-negotiable to cover all five where you can — it's important and fundamental to this stage of the sale. At minimum, you must have goal (Q1), frequency (Q4) and start date (Q5) before moving to Stage 3.

The Five Questions
What you're learning: Why they're here. This is your foundation question — their answer tells you what matters most to them and shapes everything that comes after. Here are some common responses and how to handle them.
What they say
"I want to tone up / core strength / mobility"
What it means
Goal stated but vague — you don't have enough to work with yet
What you do
Acknowledge it — then drill down to get a real picture
Drill Down
  • "What would that actually look like for you day to day?" Gets them picturing the real outcome rather than the label.
  • "What's been stopping you from getting there until now?" Surfaces the real barrier early — feeds directly into your recommendation.
What they say
"I've never done Pilates before"
What it means
Statement of inexperience — not a goal. You still don't know why they're here.
What you do
Reassure them, then find the actual motivation underneath
Drill Down
  • "That's totally fine — what made you want to give it a go?" Redirects from the statement back to the real reason they're calling.
What they say
"I need something low-impact"
What it means
Constraint identified — but you still don't know the actual goal
What you do
Find out what they're trying to achieve within that constraint
Drill Down
  • "Makes sense — is that because of an injury or just a preference?" Separates medical from lifestyle — affects how you pitch and what you flag.
  • "And what are you hoping to get out of it — strength, flexibility, something else?" Gets the actual goal now that you know the constraint.
What they say
"I want to get stronger"
What it means
Clear goal — but "stronger" means different things to different people
What you do
Get the why and the picture behind it before recommending
Drill Down
  • "Love that — stronger in what sense? Functional day-to-day strength, or more of a body composition thing?" Helps you target the right class types and frame the recommendation correctly.
What they say
"Someone told me / I walked past / saw it online"
What it means
No motivation given — referral or passive discovery. Cold lead.
What you do
Full drill down — you have nothing to work with yet
Drill Down
  • "Oh nice — what was it that caught your attention / what did they say about it?" Finds out what angle got them interested — use that as your hook.
  • "And is there something specific you're hoping it could help you with?" Gets the actual goal — don't assume anything with a cold lead.
What they say
"I used to go to [gym] but..."
What it means
Past experience that didn't stick — something went wrong
What you do
Unpack what didn't work — that's your positioning opportunity
Drill Down
  • "What was it that didn't work for you there?" Surfaces the real reason they left — position STRONG as the answer to exactly that.
  • "And what are you hoping is different this time?" Lets them tell you what they need — you just have to confirm STRONG delivers it.
Rule
Never accept a surface answer on Q1. Even a clear goal like "get stronger" needs depth — what does that mean for them, why now, what's stood in the way. You're building the case for your recommendation here.
What you're learning: Where they're starting from. This tells you how to pitch the product and whether there are any barriers around their body or schedule that need to be addressed. Here are some common responses and how to handle them.
What they say
"Not really, I'm new to this"
What it means
Complete beginner — may be nervous about walking in cold
What you do
Reassure — our classes are coached, not self-directed. Mention Starter Class if hesitant
Drill Down
  • "That's totally fine — our classes are fully coached so you're never just figuring it out on your own. Have you done anything similar before, like yoga or reformer Pilates?" Reassures while gathering more context on their baseline.
What they say
"I used to but I stopped"
What it means
Lapsed exerciser — something caused them to fall off
What you do
Find out why they stopped — that's your positioning opportunity
Drill Down
  • "What got in the way?" Simple and open — lets them tell you exactly what the barrier was.
What they say
"I do yoga / running / weights"
What it means
Active — looking to add something or switch
What you do
Position STRONG as a complement (strength + structure) or upgrade (more results-driven)
Drill Down
  • "And is STRONG something you'd want to add on top of that, or are you looking to switch things up?" Clarifies how STRONG fits into their existing routine — shapes how you frame the recommendation.
What they say
"I have a [injury / condition]"
What it means
Physical limitation — need to establish clearance and expectation
What you do
Ask if they've been cleared by a doctor — note modifications are available
Drill Down
  • "Have you been cleared to exercise by a doctor or physio?" Worth asking — not to gatekeep, but to make sure they're set up for a good experience from day one.
  • "Our coaches can work around most things — it's worth having a chat with them before your first class so they know." Reassures without over-promising. Sets the right expectation.
What they say
"I used to do Pilates / Lagree / reformer"
What it means
Experienced — knows the format, doesn't need basics explained
What you do
Move faster — focus on what makes STRONG different, not what Pilates is
Drill Down
  • "So you'll feel comfortable on the equipment straight away. What made you want to switch it up / try something new?" Skips the basics, gets back to motivation — keeps the pace moving.
What they say
"I go to the gym / lift weights"
What it means
Strength-focused — may question whether STRONG adds anything
What you do
Position STRONG as the complement — not a replacement
Drill Down
  • "A lot of our members come from a gym background — they find STRONG fills in the gaps. Are you looking to add this alongside what you're doing, or thinking of mixing things up?" Validates their background while opening the door to a conversation about fit.
What they say
"I play sport / I'm in pre/off-season"
What it means
Athletic — looking for performance support or recovery
What you do
Position STRONG as conditioning and injury prevention alongside sport
Drill Down
  • "What sport? And is this something you'd want to do alongside training, or mainly in the off-season?" Gets the context to frame STRONG as a performance tool, not just fitness.
What they say
"I currently do reformer Pilates somewhere else"
What it means
Active reformer user — comparing or considering switching
What you do
Find out what's prompting the look — don't speak negatively of their current studio
Drill Down
  • "What made you want to check us out?" Simple and open — lets them reveal whether they're shopping around or ready to move.
Red Flags — Proceed with Care
If they mention a recent injury or chronic pain — ask if they think it's something they should get cleared with a doctor first. If they're unsure, encourage them to do so unless they feel like it will be fine. Let them know modifications are available once they're ready. Don't push someone into booking when they may not be in a position to train safely so just be mindful and use your judgement.

Skip Q2 if: their answer to Q1 already made their experience level clear. Don't ask questions you already know the answer to.

What you're learning: What's stood in the way before. This is the most important question for reducing late-stage objections. If you know their barrier now, you can address it before it becomes a reason not to sign up. Here are some common responses and how to handle them.
💡
The goal here isn't to fix the objection on the spot — it's to acknowledge it and note it. You'll come back to it when you make your recommendation. The lead feels heard, and you already have your answer ready when it comes up again.
What they say
"Cost — it's expensive"
What it means
Price sensitivity — they're already thinking about money before you've pitched
What you do
Note it — you'll address value and cost-per-class in the recommendation. Don't defend price now.
Drill Down
  • "Yeah, I get that — we'll go through everything and make sure whatever we recommend actually makes sense for you. Can I ask, what's your goal with it?" Acknowledges without defending. Redirects back to discovery.
What they say
"Time — I'm too busy"
What it means
Schedule concern — often a surface reason. The real issue is usually consistency, not hours.
What you do
Find out when they actually are free — often the schedule isn't the real issue
Drill Down
  • "Totally understand — what does your week normally look like? Are there any windows that tend to be more consistent?" Turns a vague barrier into a scheduling conversation — much easier to solve.
What they say
"I always start then stop"
What it means
Commitment and follow-through issues — they know their own pattern
What you do
Position the Intro Offer as low-risk — they're not locking into anything long-term
Drill Down
  • "What do you think gets in the way when that happens?" Gets the specific reason — you can position the Intro as a way to test commitment without the pressure of a membership.
What they say
"I felt out of place / intimidated at [gym]"
What it means
Past bad experience — environment or culture wasn't right for them
What you do
Emphasise the coached environment, the community, beginner-friendly format
Drill Down
  • "That's really common — what was it about the environment that didn't feel right?" Lets them articulate the specific issue so you can directly address it.
What they say
"I hate contracts"
What it means
Contract aversion — often from a bad experience being locked in somewhere
What you do
Lead with the Intro Offer — note no-contract membership options exist
Drill Down
  • "Completely understandable — we actually have options with no contract at all, and the Intro Offer is just a one-off payment. We can go through those when we get to that part." Neutralises the objection early without going into full pitch mode.
What they say
"Nothing really"
What it means
No major barriers — great sign, they're genuinely ready
What you do
Move forward with confidence — don't create doubt where there isn't any
Drill Down
  • "Perfect — sounds like you're ready to go." Affirm it and move on. Don't dig for problems that aren't there.
What you're learning: Which product fits them. Frequency is the single most important factor in your recommendation — you cannot present options without it. Here are some common responses and how to handle them.
What they say
"Once a week"
What it means
Low frequency — but still a commitment. Don't dismiss it.
What you do
Acknowledge it as a real starting point — then explore if they'd consider building from there
Drill Down
  • "Once a week is a solid start — is that because of schedule or just where you want to begin?" Opens the door to a higher frequency without pushing. If it's schedule, you can problem-solve. If it's preference, respect it.
What they say
"Twice a week"
What it means
Clear, realistic commitment — the most common answer
What you do
Intro Offer → 2/week Membership. Straightforward path.
Drill Down
  • "Two is a great starting point — consistent enough to actually feel the difference." Affirm it and move on. No need to push higher.
What they say
"Three or more times"
What it means
High commitment — results-focused, motivated
What you do
Intro Offer → Unlimited Membership. 2/week caps them.
Drill Down
  • "That's great — at that frequency you'd want to look at the Unlimited so you're not capped. We'll come back to that." Plants the seed for Unlimited without getting into price yet.
What they say
"As much as possible"
What it means
Very motivated — but needs a real number to work with
What you do
Pin down a realistic frequency — then path to Unlimited
Drill Down
  • "Love the enthusiasm — realistically, what does your week look like? Are we talking 3 times, 4?" Gets a real number so you can match the right product.
What they say
"Not sure / it depends on my schedule"
What it means
Uncertain — needs a frame to work from
What you do
Give them one — anchor to 2–3 as a starting point
Drill Down
  • "No worries — 2 to 3 sessions a week is a great starting point for most people. It gives you consistency without overloading your schedule. Does that sound doable for you?" Gives them a frame without pressure. Most people say yes — and you have your frequency.
Rule
Never skip Q4. Frequency determines the recommendation. Without it, you're guessing — and guessing leads to the wrong product and objections you can't answer.
What you're learning: How ready they are right now. Urgency shapes how you present the recommendation and what language you use in the close. Here are some common responses and how to handle them.
What they say
"ASAP / this week"
What it means
High urgency — they're ready right now
What you do
Move to recommendation immediately — don't slow down
Drill Down
  • "Perfect — let's sort that out now." Match their energy. Don't pad it out.
What they say
"Next week / soon"
What it means
Moderate urgency — interested but hasn't fully committed yet
What you do
Nail down a specific date — vague timelines go cold
Drill Down
  • "What day works for you to get started?" Turns "soon" into a real commitment. A specific day is a next step.
What they say
"After [holiday / event / thing]"
What it means
Committed but delayed — has a real timeline in mind
What you do
Note the date, confirm you'll follow up closer to then
Drill Down
  • "No problem — when is that? I'll reach out closer to the time and we can get you booked in." Creates a follow-up touchpoint. Don't just leave it hanging.
What they say
"I want to try a class first"
What it means
Wants to test before committing — reasonable hesitation
What you do
Offer Starter Class — book them in before ending the call
Drill Down
  • "Absolutely — we actually run a free Starter Class on Saturday mornings at 11am specifically for that. Want me to book you in?" Turns hesitation into a booked appointment. Don't leave it as "come when you want".
What they say
"Not sure / when I can"
What it means
Low urgency or avoidance — there's often a real reason underneath
What you do
Probe gently — don't accept it at face value
Drill Down
  • "What would need to change for you to get started soon?" Opens up the real barrier — often it's cost, schedule, or confidence. All of which you can address.
Rule
Never skip Q5. A start date creates commitment. Without one, you're ending a call with no next step — and leads without next steps go cold.
Best Practices

Let them talk. Your job in discovery is to ask and listen — not to fill silence. If they pause, let them. They're thinking, and what comes next is usually the most useful thing they'll tell you.

Don't rapid-fire the questions. Discovery should feel like a conversation, not an intake form. Ask one question, listen to the full answer, respond naturally, then move to the next. If an answer naturally covers two questions, skip ahead — don't ask something they've already answered.

Use bridges to keep the conversation moving: "Got it", "That makes sense", "I hear you." These signal that you're listening without interrupting the flow. Don't over-acknowledge — one bridge per response is enough.

Take notes. Goals, barriers, experience level, schedule, anything that suggests budget sensitivity, past gym experiences. You'll use these notes when you make your recommendation — referencing what they told you is what makes the pitch feel personal rather than generic.

Rapport doesn't come from small talk — it comes from genuine curiosity. A lead who feels genuinely understood is far more likely to trust your recommendation.

Discovery Completion Checklist
⚠️
Before moving to Stage 3 — confirm you have all five:
Primary goal/motivation · Fitness experience level · Any barriers or past failures · Ideal weekly session frequency · Intended start date. If you're missing any of these, ask before recommending. At minimum you need goal, frequency and start date.
Call Notes Template

Copy this before every discovery call and paste it into Hapana Grow as your call notes. Fill in the answers as you go.

Discovery Call Notes

Discovery Questions

What made you look into STRONG?
Answer:
2
Are you doing any training at the moment?
Answer:
3
Has anything stopped you from starting before?
Answer:
4
How many sessions a week would you ideally want to do?
Answer:
5
When were you thinking of getting started?
Answer:
Objections / Roadblocks
Notes:
Outcome
Recommended:

Incentive offered:
Follow-up required:
Notes:
Chapter 02 — Products & Offers / Products, Pricing & Offers

Products, Pricing & Offers

Sales Reference Location Specific
Before You Present Anything
Discovery must be complete before presenting options. If you haven't confirmed frequency and start date, go back. You cannot recommend the right option without knowing what the prospect needs.
STRONG Intro Offer
STRONG Intro Offer
5 Sessions · 14 Days · First-time members only
$50
One-time payment
What they get
5 sessions, any class, any equipment. Expires 14 days from first class booked — whichever comes first.
Who it's for
First-time members only. Never been to a STRONG class before. Always the starting point — not a membership.
Incentive rules
No discounts or incentives permitted. Escalate to management for unique scenarios. Re-Intro Offer available for members returning after extended absence.
Current Promotions
STRONG Starter Class
  • Free · Beginners only — Use for hesitant leads not ready to commit to the Intro Offer. Lower barrier entry before the paid intro.
  • Never position the Starter Class as a free trial of the Intro Offer. It's a standalone entry point for people who need to experience STRONG before they decide.
Memberships
2 Sessions / Week
Membership · No contract
$50
/week
Who it's for
Prospect wants exactly 2 sessions/week with a definite schedule. Great starting point if unsure about frequency — can upgrade anytime.
Terms
No lock-in — cancel anytime with 21 days' notice
Pause up to 30 days a year
No rollover for session credits
If hesitant
1 First week free — offer this first. Only if hesitant.
— No price discount available on this membership
Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount. Stacking permitted as final play.
STRONG Human Unlimited (6-Month)
Membership · 6-Month Contract
$70
/week
Who it's for
Prospect wants 3+ sessions/week and is comfortable committing to 6 months. Best weekly rate for unlimited access.
Terms
6-month commitment — early exit fees apply
Cancel after your term with 21 days' notice
Pause up to 30 days a year — pause time added to your end date
If hesitant — offer in order
1 First week free — offer this first
2 If declined: $65/week discounted rate
Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount. Stacking permitted as final play.
STRONG Human Unlimited
Membership · No Contract
$75
/week
Who it's for
Prospect wants 3+ sessions/week but reluctant to commit to a contract. Flexibility is the priority — worth the extra $5/week.
Terms
No lock-in — cancel anytime with 21 days' notice
Pause up to 30 days a year
If hesitant — offer in order
1 First week free — offer this first
2 If declined: $70/week discounted rate
Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount. Stacking permitted as final play.
STRONG Human Passport
Membership · Multi-location · No contract
$80
/week
Who it's for
Prospect asks about training across multiple STRONG locations. Present only when asked — do not volunteer.
Terms
No lock-in — cancel anytime with 21 days' notice
Pause up to 30 days a year
Included locations
If hesitant — offer in order
1 First week free — offer this first
2 If declined: $75/week discounted rate
Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount. Stacking permitted as final play.
Only mention if the prospect asks about training at multiple locations.
Product Mapping
1–2 / week
Non-committal
↓
Class Pack
2/week membership if they want structure and sound definite about twice a week
Exactly 2 / week
Definite
↓
2/week Membership
Class pack only if they need flexibility around a fixed schedule
2–3 / week
Uncertain
↓
2/week or Unlimited
Ask: more likely to do 2 or 3 per week? If unsure, start with 2/week — can upgrade to Unlimited anytime
3+ / week
High frequency
↓
Unlimited Membership
Best value at this frequency — no reason to consider a pack
When to offer a pack instead — memberships are always the priority
→They say "I travel a lot" or "my shifts change week to week"
→They say "I just want something flexible" or ask for a pack directly
→They say 1–2 sessions but sound non-committal about when or how often
→They mention difficulty committing to a regular schedule

Private Sessions
Private & Semi-Private Sessions
45 minutes · Instructor-led · Tailored to you · Outside regular class times
Who books privates?
  • Wants direct, tailored one-on-one guidance
  • Couples or small groups training together
  • Prefers privacy over a class environment
  • 16+ weeks pregnant (with doctor's approval)
Session details
45 minutes, fully instructor-led
Any STRONG equipment — tailored to the member
Sessions run outside existing class times
Time arranged directly with a trainer
How to Book
Trainer on site
Trainer organises time directly. FOH administers the sale.
FOH only
FOH liaises with trainer to confirm time. FOH administers the sale.
Neither available
Refer to Customer Care — they liaise with trainer and administer the sale.
⚠ Payment cannot be taken until session time is confirmed with a trainer.
1:1 Private Packages
Package
Price
Per Session
If hesitant
1 Session
Single session · No expiry
$75
$75.00
5-Session Package
Save vs single
$350
$70.00/session
10-Session Package
Best per-session rate
$650
$65.00/session
Semi-Private & Group Sessions
Format
Total Price
Per Person
If hesitant
2:1 Semi-Private
2 people · 1 trainer
$130
$65.00/person
3:1 Semi-Private
3 people · 1 trainer
$180
$60.00/person
4:1 Semi-Private
4 people · 1 trainer
$220
$55.00/person
Private Group (5+)
5 or more · up to location max beds
$250
$50/person at 5
reduces with group size
Private session discounts are not standardised. Any discount must be escalated to management for review and approval — unless blanket pre-approval has been given for your location. Never offer a discount without authorisation.
Sauna Packages
Sauna Packages
Add-on · Session-based
1 Session
$35
Expires 30 days
3 Sessions
$99
Expires 30 days
5 Pack
$159
Expires 40 days
No discounts on sauna packages typically — any exceptions require manager review and approval. Some memberships include a complimentary sauna entitlement — check location config. Purchased sessions stack on top.
Incentive Rules
The Golden Rules
  • Always quote retail pricing first — never lead with discounts
  • Only offer incentives if prospect shows hesitation or uncertainty
  • Incentive sequence: Retail → Free week → Discount → Free week + Discount (stacking permitted as final play)
  • For class packs: offer value-add (bonus classes) first, then discount if declined.
  • Offer primary incentive first — move to discount only if primary is declined
Group Sign-Ups
Same incentive structure applies regardless of how many people are joining. Do not offer a group discount. Same process, same rules.
Important
Do not offer the free week AND the discount rate right away. If a prospect declines the free week, the discount can then be offered.
Quick Reference
Weekly Sessions
Recommended Product
1–2 / week
Non-committal
Class Pack
Exactly 2 / week
Definite
2/week Membership
2–3 / week
Uncertain
2/week or Unlimited — ask which is more likely
3+ / week
High frequency
Unlimited Membership

Price
Terms

STRONG Intro Offer
$50
5 sessions · 14 days · one-time

Memberships
